mainTopic: (continues) עודו הטל עליהן ושמח · אבידה ששטפה נהר · הלכה כאבוי ביעל קגם
overview: (1) The Gemara analyzes a case where the owner rejoices while dew is still on the produce, discussing whether this constitutes valid ייאוש based on the verse 'כי יותן'. (2) Rabbi Yochanan cites Rabbi Shimon ben Yehotzadak deriving that a lost item swept by a river is permitted, and the Gemara notes this contradicts Rav Acha's earlier ruling. (3) The Gemara states the halacha follows Abaye regarding 'Ya'al Kagem' and discusses Rav Ashi's ruling on eating dates infested with insects, and the status of items found in an orphan's field. (4) A debate between Rava and Rav Ashi regarding whether a mark made to be trampled (סמא העשוי לידרס) is considered a valid siman, and their respective explanations of the Mishnah regarding bundles in public vs. private domains. — discussion continues into next amud.

sugyaBoundaries.gist: The Gemara analyzes a case where the owner rejoices while dew is still on the produce, discussing whether this constitutes valid ייאוש based on the verse 'כי יותן'.
Rabbi Yochanan cites Rabbi Shimon ben Yehotzadak deriving that a lost item swept by a river is permitted, and the Gemara notes this contradicts Rav Acha's earlier ruling.
The Gemara states the halacha follows Abaye regarding 'Ya'al Kagem' and discusses Rav Ashi's ruling on eating dates infested with insects, and the status of items found in an orphan's field.

A debate between Rava and Rav Ashi regarding whether a mark made to be trampled (סמא העשוי לידרס) is considered a valid siman, and their respective explanations of the Mishnah regarding bundles in public vs. private domains. — discussion continues into next amud.

steps.title: Questioning the Logic of the Distinction
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא asks why the distinction exists, suggesting it might be because we don't say that if he is happy now, he was happy from the start.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא probes the underlying logic of the ברייתא. It asks if the reason is that we do not assume retrospective ייאוש.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This שאלה tests the assumption that the ברייתa supports concurrent ייאוש. It implies that if retrospective happiness counted, the distinction between wet and dry dew would be unnecessary.

keyTerms.meaning: He was also amenable from the outset

phrases.aramaic: טַעְמָא מַאי? לָאו מִשּׁוּם דְּלָא אָמְרִינַן כֵּיוָן דְּאִיגַּלַּאי
phrases.english: What is the reason? Is it not because we do not say: Since it was revealed
A matter that now is pleasing to him,
From the beginning also is pleasing to him?

phrases.aramaic: שָׁאנֵי הָתָם, דִּכְתִיב ״כִּי יִתֵּן״. עַד שֶׁיִּתֵּן.
phrases.english: There is different, as it is written 'When he places'. Until he places.

steps.triggerLanguage: טַעְמָא מַאי? לָאו מִשּׁוּם דְּלָא אָמְרִינַן כֵּיוָן דְּאִיגַּלַּאי מִילְּתָא דְּהַשְׁתָּא נִיחָא לֵיהּ, מֵעִקָּרָא נָמֵי נִיחָא לֵיהּ?

steps.title: Challenge: The First Clause Lacks Active Placement
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא challenges the תירוץ by noting that the first clause (dew still on produce) also lacks active placement by the owner.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא raises a קשיא against the proposed תירוץ. If the verse requires active placement, why does the first clause (natural dew) count as 'כי יותן'?
steps.deeperAnalysis: This קשיא attacks the תירוץ by showing it doesn't fit the first clause. Since dew falls naturally, not by the owner's hand, the explanation of active placement seems insufficient.

phrases.aramaic: אִי הָכִי, רֵישָׁא נָמֵי: הָתָם כִּדְרַב פָּפָּא.
phrases.english: If so, the beginning too: there according to Rav Pappa.
That Rav Pappa raises,
It is written 'ki yitten',
And we read ki yutan! How then?

steps.triggerLanguage: אִי הָכִי, רֵישָׁא נָמֵי: הָתָם כִּדְרַב פָּפָּא.

rashi.hebrew: כתיב כי יתן - ולא כתיב כי יותן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the verse explicitly uses the active form 'כי ייתן' rather than the passive 'כי יותן', establishing that the Torah requires the owner's active placement of the item.

steps.title: Resolution: Rav Pappa's Textual Analysis
steps.stepSummary: The גמרא answers by citing Rav Pappa, who explains that the verse is written 'כי ייתן' but read 'כי יותן', requiring similarity between the two.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא provides a תירוץ by bringing Rav Pappa's explanation. Rav Pappa reconciles the written form 'כי ייתן' with the read form 'כי יותן' by requiring similarity in the owner's knowledge.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This תירוץ resolves the קשיא by showing how the verse applies to natural dew. The owner's awareness at the time of the event is the key factor, not active physical placement.

keyTerms.meaning: And we read 'when it is placed'

phrases.english: We require 'ki yuttan' to resemble 'ki yitten'.

phrases.aramaic: מָה ״יִתֵּן״ לְדַעַת, אַף ״כִּי יוּתַּן״ נָמֵי לְדַעַת.
phrases.english: What is 'yitten' by knowledge, also 'ki yuttan' also by knowledge.

steps.triggerLanguage: דְּרַב פָּפָּא רָמֵי, כְּתִיב ״כִּי יִתֵּן״, וְקָרֵינַן כִּי יוּתַּן! הָא כֵּיצַד? בָּעֵינַן ״כִּי יוּתַּן״ דֻּומְיָא ״דְּכִי יִתֵּן״. מָה ״יִתֵּן״ לְדַעַת, אַף ״כִּי יוּתַּן״ נָמֵי לְדַעַת.

rashi.hebrew: לדעת - שידע בנתינה והוכשר בעיניו ועודהו הטל עליהן עדיין הן בנתינתו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that לדעת means the owner was aware and satisfied with the deposit while the dew was still on the produce.

steps.title: רבי יוחנן's Teaching on River-Swept Items
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara introduces a teaching of רבי יוחנן in the name of רבי ישמעאל בן יוֹצדק, deriving from a verse that a lost item swept away by a river is permitted for the finder to keep.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא brings a new source to test רבא's ruling. This is a freestanding teaching (מימרא) introduced by רבי יוחנן.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The source derives from דברים that an item swept by a river is permitted to keep. The verse excludes items lost from the owner but not available to be found by anyone.

keyTerms.meaning: A lost item swept away by a river
Which is lost from him and you have found it (the verse)
Available to be found by any person

phrases.aramaic: תָּא שְׁמַע דְּאָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן מִשּׁוּם
phrases.english: Come and hear that which Rabbi Yoḥanan says in the name of
Rabbi Yishmael ben Yehotzadak:

phrases.aramaic: מִנַּיִן לַאֲבֵידָה שֶׁשְּׁטָפָהּ נָהָר שֶׁהִיא מוּתֶּרֶת? דִּכְתִיב:
phrases.english: From where is it derived for a lost item that a river swept away that it is…
“And so shall you do with his donkey; and so shall you do with his…

phrases.aramaic: וְכֵן תַּעֲשֶׂה לְכׇל אֲבֵידַת אָחִיךָ אֲשֶׁר
phrases.english: and so shall you do with every lost item of your brother which
shall be lost from him, and you have found it.”

phrases.aramaic: מִי שֶׁאֲבוּדָה הֵימֶנּוּ וּמְצוּיָה אֵצֶל כׇּל אָדָם,
phrases.english: That which is lost from him and is found by every person,

phrases.aramaic: יָצָאתָה זוֹ שֶׁאֲבוּדָה מִמֶּנּוּ וְאֵינָהּ מְצוּיָה
phrases.english: excluded is this one that is lost from him and is not found
by every person.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּא שְׁמַע דְּאָמַר רַבִּי יוֹחָנָן מִשּׁוּם רַבִּי יִשְׁמָעֵאל בֶּן יְהוֹצָדָק:

rashi.hebrew: ממנו - קרא יתירא הוא לאשמועינן שאינו אבוד אלא הימנו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the word ממנו in the verse is an extra word used to teach that the item is only considered lost from the owner's perspective, not from the perspective of the general public.

steps.title: Comparison of Prohibition and Permission
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara extends the ruling via a comparison (דומיא) between the permitted case and the forbidden case, concluding that even an item with a distinguishing mark swept by a river is forbidden.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא analyzes the verse further using a דומיא (comparison) between the prohibition and the permission derived from it.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The verse prohibits keeping an item that is lost but findable. The Gemara compares this prohibition to the permission derived earlier, extending the ruling to items with סימנים as well.

keyTerms.meaning: The prohibition is compared to the permission

keyTerms.term: בֵּין דְּאִית בַּהּ סִימָן וּבֵין דְּלֵית בַּהּ סִימָן
keyTerms.meaning: Whether there is a distinguishing mark or not

phrases.english: And the prohibition resembles the permission,

phrases.aramaic: מָה הֶיתֵּירָא בֵּין דְּאִית בַּהּ סִימָן
phrases.english: What is the permission? Between that which has a distinguishing mark
and that which does not have a distinguishing mark – it is permitted,

phrases.aramaic: אַף אִיסּוּרָא בֵּין דְּאִית בַּהּ סִימַן
phrases.english: so too the prohibition, between that which has a distinguishing mark
and that which does not have a distinguishing mark –
it is prohibited. A refutation of Rava, a refutation!

rashi.hebrew: ואיסורא דומיא דהיתירא - כיון דמחד קרא ילפינן מצויה אצל כל אדם שיחזור ושאינה מצויה יזכה בה איתקושי אתקוש להדדי זו לאיסורא וזו להיתירא:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that since the verse teaches both the prohibition of a findable item and the permission of an unfindable item, these two categories are compared to each other through a דומיא.

rashi.hebrew: מה היתירא - שאינה מצויה לא חילק בין שיש בה סימן לשאין בה סימן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the permission for an unfindable item does not distinguish between items with a סימן and those without.

rashi.hebrew: אף איסורא - דמצויה אצל כל אדם לא חילק בין אין סימן דכי ידע מיאש ליש סימן דלכי ידע לא מיאש וכי היכי דיש סימן אסירא כי אין סימן נמי אסירא היכא דאיכא למימר לא ידע דנפלה קודם שבאת ליד זה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the prohibition for a findable item also does not distinguish based on a סימן, meaning it is forbidden even with a סימן if the owner did not know it fell and thus did not despair before the finder acquired it.

tosafot.hebrew: איסורא דומיא דהתירא - מה שמתיר אבודה ממנו ומכל אדם ע"כ לא איצטריך קרא בידע ומתיאש דהא אפילו במצויין אצל כל אדם מותרת ביאוש אלא לא אצטריך אלא כי לא ידע ואע"ג דלכי ידע לא מתיאש אפי' אם יש בו סימן כיון שהיא אבודה מכל אדם ה"נ איסורא דהיינו מצוייה אצל כל אדם דאסור כה"ג דלא ידעינן דמיאש בין יש בו סימן בין שאין בו סימן ואין להתיר אלא דמיאש קודם דאתא לידיה דשריא רחמנא:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the verse is not needed to permit an item lost from everyone with ייאוש, but rather to permit an item lost from everyone even without ייאוש, and similarly the prohibition applies to findable items regardless of a סימן when we do not know if ייאוש occurred before acquisition.

tosafot.hebrew: תיובתא דרבא - רבא ידע שפיר הך ברייתא כדמשמע קצת לעיל (בבא מציעא דף כא:) דקאמר בזוטו של ים כ"ע לא פליגי דשרי כדבעינן למימר לקמן אלא דלא דייק איסורא דומיא דהתירא:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות clarifies that רבא was aware of this ברייתא, as implied earlier regarding items swept by the sea, but simply did not analyze the דומיא of איסורא דומיא דהיתירא.

steps.title: The הלכה of אַבּוּי יַעַל קַגֶּם
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara states that the halacha follows Abaye in the disputes covered by the mnemonic 'Abui Ya'al Kagem'.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא concludes the previous sugya by stating the final הלכה regarding the mnemonic אַבּוּי יַעַל קַגֶּם. This serves as a transition marker, closing the prior argument and setting the stage for new questions.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This is a standard הלכה conclusion following a debate between אַבָּיֵי and רַבָּא. The mnemonic lists specific topics where the הלכה follows אַבָּיֵי despite the general rule of הלכה כרבה. This establishes the baseline that ייאוש שכלו אינו ייאוש.

keyTerms.meaning: Mnemonic for disputes where הלכה follows אַבָּיֵי
Refers to ייאוש שכלו אינו ייאוש

steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the baseline הלכה that ייאוש שכלו אינו ייאוש, which is the premise for רַב אַחָא's subsequent questions.

phrases.english: And the halakha is in accordance with Abaye regarding a lost item.

rashi.hebrew: יע"ל קג"ם - סימני הלכות הן יאוש שלא מדעת דהכא עד זומם למפרע הוא נפסל בסנהדרין (דף כז.) לחי העומד מאיליו בעירובין (דף טו.) קדושין שלא נמסרו לביאה בקדושין (דף נא.) גלוי דעתא בגיטא בגיטין (דף לד.) מומר אוכל נבילות להכעיס פסול לענין עדות בסנהדרין (דף כז.):
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that the mnemonic אבוי יעל קגם refers to six specific halachic rulings where the law follows אבא, including יאוש שלא מדעת, עד זומם, לחי העומד מאיליו, קדושין שלא נמסרו, גלוי דעתא בגיטא, and מומר אוכל נבילות.

steps.title: How do we eat תַּמְרֵי דְזִיקָא?
steps.stepSummary: Rav Acha asks Rav Ashi how we can eat dates blown off by the wind if unconscious despair is not considered despair.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב אַחָא raises a practical difficulty based on the new הלכה. If ייאוש שכלו אינו ייאוש, then the owner of the dates has not despaired, and eating them should be forbidden as theft.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This is a שאלה, not a קשיא, because it asks for the practical application of the new ruling rather than attacking the text. רַב אַחָא asks how we justify eating these dates if the owner did not despair. The underlying assumption is that without ייאוש, the dates remain the owner's property.

keyTerms.meaning: Dates blown off by the wind

steps.whyThisMatters: This question forces the גמרא to find a new basis for acquiring found items when the owner's despair is not conscious.

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב אַחָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרָבָא לְרַב אָשֵׁי:
phrases.english: Rav Aḥa, son of Rava, said to Rav Ashi:
And now that the opinion of Rava was refuted –

phrases.aramaic: הָנֵי תַּמְרֵי דְזִיקָא הֵיכִי אָכְלִינַן לְהוּ? אֲמַר לֵיהּ:
phrases.english: these dates blown by the wind, how do we eat them? He said to him:

phrases.aramaic: כֵּיוָן דְּאִיכָּא שְׁקָצִים וּרְמָשִׂים דְּקָא אָכְלִי לְהוּ,
phrases.english: Since there are repugnant creatures and creeping animals that are eating them,
from the beginning, the owner despairs of them.

steps.triggerLanguage: אֲמַר לֵיהּ רַב אַחָא בְּרֵיהּ דְּרָבָא לְרַב אָשֵׁי: וְכִי מֵאַחַר דְּאִיתּוֹתַב רָבָא – הָנֵי תַּמְרֵי דְזִיקָא הֵיכִי אָכְלִינַן לְהוּ?

rashi.hebrew: הני תמרי דזיקא - שהרוח משיר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that תמרי דזיקא refers to dates that the wind has stripped from the tree.

rashi.hebrew: היכי אכלינן להו - הא לא מיאש והא דנקט למיבעי בעיא מאחר דאיתותב רבא קשיא לי דבלא איתותב רבא איכא למיבעי דהא מודה רבא בכל דבר שחזותו מוכיח עליו חוץ מן התאנה מפני שנמאסת עם נפילתה ונראה בעיני דלא נקט לה אלא להרבות בחומר איסור כלומר מאחר דאיתותב אפילו בדבר שאין בו סימן כ"ש שאלו אסורין שיש בהן סימן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that Rav Acha specifically asks this question after רבא's ruling to emphasize the severity of the prohibition, implying that if it is forbidden even for items without a distinguishing mark (like dates), it is certainly forbidden for items with a mark.

rashi.hebrew: מעיקרא - מקודם נפילתו:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that מעיקרא refers to the time before the dates fell from the tree.

rashi.hebrew: יאושי מיאש - דיודע הוא שהרוח משיר מהן והשקצים מזומנים לאוכלן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that יאושי מיאש means the owner knows the wind will strip the dates and insects are ready to eat them, so he despaired from the start.

tosafot.hebrew: מאחר דאיתותב רבא כו' - בשלמא לרבא אע"ג דאותם שתחת האילן או בסמוך לאילן אסירי דחזותו מוכיח עליו אבל תמרי שהרוח מוליכן ברחוק דלא שייך חזותו מוכיח עליו אע"פ שבעלים סבורין שיפלו תחת האילן מ"מ אם היו יודעין שהרוח מוליכן מרחוק היו מתיאשין אלא לאביי הא הוי יאוש שלא מדעת שהבעלים סבורים שיפלו תחת האילן ולא יקחום עוברי דרכים משום דחזותו מוכיח עליו ומשני דאפילו אותן שתחת האילן שרו אפילו לאביי באתרא דשכיחי שקצים ורמשים דמיאש וצ"ל דעבידי דנתרי כמו תאנים דאל"כ היו אסורים לאביי דהוי יאוש שלא מדעת דאינו סובר שיפלו כיון דלא עבידי דנתרי כמו זתים וחרובים דאסור כיון דלא עבידי דנתרי:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that according to רבא, dates blown far away are permitted because the owner would have despaired if he knew the wind would carry them far, whereas according to אביי, even dates under the tree are permitted in places where insects are common because the owner despaired, assuming dates are like figs that rot and are thus abandoned.

steps.title: What if the dates belong to יַתְמֵי?
steps.stepSummary: Rav Acha asks what happens if the dates belong to orphans, who cannot despair.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב אַחָא raises a new difficulty. יַתְמֵי are not בְּנֵי מְחִילָה, so they cannot despair. If the dates belong to יַתְמֵי, רַב אָשֵׁי's answer doesn't work.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This is a שאלה. רַב אַחָא is testing the limits of רַב אָשֵׁי's answer. If the owner is an orphan, the concept of ייאוש doesn't apply because יַתְמֵי cannot relinquish their property. Therefore, the dates should remain forbidden. רַב אַחָא asks how we justify eating dates if they might belong to יַתְמֵי.
steps.whyThisMatters: This question highlights the unique status of יַתְמֵי property and the need for a different mechanism to acquire items found in their fields.

phrases.aramaic: יַתְמֵי דְּלָאו בְּנֵי מְחִילָה נִינְהוּ, מַאי?
phrases.english: The orphans who are not heirs, what?

phrases.aramaic: אֲמַר לֵיהּ: בָּאגָא בְּאַרְעָא דְיַתְמֵי לָא מַחְזְקִינַן.
phrases.english: He said to him: In the field of orphans we do not establish possession.

steps.triggerLanguage: יַתְמֵי דְּלָאו בְּנֵי מְחִילָה נִינְהוּ, מַאי?

rashi.hebrew: יתמי - קטנים שאין הפקירן הפקר:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines יתומים as קטנים who lack the legal capacity to effectuate הפקר, meaning their property cannot be relinquished.

rashi.hebrew: באגא בארעא דיתמי לא מחזקינן - אין עלינו להחזיק כל הבקעה בחזקת הקרקע של יתומין ולאסור כל התמרים משום ספק קרקע של יתומים אלא הולכין אחר הרוב:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that באגא בארעא דיתמי לא מחזקינן means we do not assume a presumption (חזקה) that the land belongs to orphans to forbid the dates, but rather we follow the majority (רוב) of landowners.

tosafot.hebrew: דלאו בני מחילה נינהו מאי - בשלמא לרבא כיון דלכי גדלי מיאשי השתא נמי מיקרי יאוש:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות notes that according to רבא, even if the orphans are currently minors, they will despair (ייאוש) when they grow up, so the concept of ייאוש still applies.

meforshim.interplaySummary: While רש״י resolves the difficulty by appealing to רוב (majority of landowners), תוספות offers an alternative resolution based on רבא's view that future ייאוש is effective.

steps.title: What if the status is מוּחְזָק or the trees are כְּרַכְתָּא?
steps.stepSummary: Rav Acha asks what happens if the trees are known to belong to orphans or are fenced off from insects.
steps.whatsHappening: רַב אַחָא presses further with two specific cases where רַב אָשֵׁי's answers don't apply. He asks about מוּחְזָק וְעוֹמֵד and כְּרַכְתָּא.
steps.deeperAnalysis: This is a שאלה. רַב אַחָא asks two questions: 1) מוּחְזָק וְעוֹמֵד — what if the status of the trees is established as belonging to יַתְמֵי? 2) כְּרַכְתָּא — what if the trees are surrounded by a fence that keeps insects out? In both cases, the previous answers are invalid.
steps.whyThisMatters: These questions test the boundaries of the הלכה and force a clear ruling on cases where the standard presumptions don't apply.

phrases.english: A well-established standing, what is it? A courtyard, what is it?
He said to him: They are forbidden.

rashi.hebrew: מוחזק ועומד מאי - קרקע עצמה של יתומין מאי:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that מוחזק ועומד refers to the land itself belonging to the orphans, establishing their ownership status.

rashi.hebrew: כרכתא מאי - דקלים הכרוכין ומוקפין בגדר של אבנים סביב שאין שקצים ורמשים נכנסין שם:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines כרכתא as date palms surrounded by a stone fence that prevents insects from entering.

tosafot.hebrew: כרכתא מאי - אם יש גדר סביב האילן מי שרו אותם שהרוח מוליך אותן חוץ לגדר דרך עליו הלא הבעלים סבורין שכולן יפלו תחת האילן ושם ישתמרו משקצים ורמשים:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות questions why the owner would despair if the wind blows dates outside the fence, since the owner assumes all dates fall under the tree and are protected from insects there.

steps.title: רַבָּה and רַבָּא's dispute on סִימָן הֶעָשׂוּי לִידָּרֵס
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara introduces the dispute between Rabba and Rava regarding whether a distinguishing mark that is prone to being trampled (סִימָן הֶעָשׂוּי לִידָּרֵס) is considered a valid siman.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא presents the fundamental הלכה dispute between רַבָּה and רַבָּא regarding the validity of a סִימָן that is likely to be destroyed by foot traffic.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבָּה rules that bundles in a public domain belong to the finder even if they have a סִימָן, implying that a סִימָן prone to being trampled is invalid because the owner despairs of it remaining intact. רַבָּא disagrees, maintaining that a סִימָן is valid even if prone to trampling, and the finder must proclaim. The גמרא notes that some teach this dispute as an independent הלכה principle.

keyTerms.meaning: A distinguishing mark that is prone to being trampled
The אָמוֹרָא who holds the סִימָן is invalid
The אָמוֹרָא who holds the סִימָן is valid

steps.whatToRemember: The core dispute is about יְאִישׁוּת regarding a סִימָן that might be destroyed.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that רַבָּה's position is derived from his ruling on bundles in a public domain, while רַבָּא's is the opposing view.
steps.whyThisMatters: This establishes the parameters of the debate before the גמרא tests it against the מִשְׁנָה and בְּרֵיתָא.

phrases.aramaic: כְּרִיכוֹת בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים – הֲרֵי אֵלּוּ שֶׁלּוֹ.
phrases.english: Bundles in a public domain — behold, these are his.

phrases.aramaic: אָמַר רַבָּה: וַאֲפִילּוּ בְּדָבָר שֶׁיֵּשׁ בּוֹ סִימָן.
phrases.english: Rabba said: And even in a matter that has a distinguishing mark.
Apparently Rabba holds:

phrases.aramaic: סִימָן הֶעָשׂוּי לִידָּרֵס לָא הָוֵי סִימָן. רָבָא אָמַר:
phrases.english: A distinguishing mark made to be trampled is not a distinguishing mark. Rava said:

phrases.aramaic: לֹא שָׁנוּ אֶלָּא בְּדָבָר שֶׁאֵין בּוֹ סִימָן,
phrases.english: They did not teach except in a matter that has no distinguishing mark,

phrases.aramaic: אֲבָל בְּדָבָר שֶׁיֵּשׁ בּוֹ סִימָן חַיָּיב לְהַכְרִיז.
phrases.english: but in a matter that has a distinguishing mark, one is obligated to proclaim.

phrases.aramaic: אַלְמָא קָסָבַר רָבָא: סִימָן הֶעָשׂוּי לִידָּרֵס הָוֵי סִימָן.
phrases.english: Apparently Rava holds: A distinguishing mark made to be trampled is a distinguishing mark.

phrases.aramaic: וְאִיכָּא דְּמַתְנֵי לְהָא שְׁמַעְתָּא בְּאַנְפֵּי נַפְשַׁהּ:
phrases.english: And there are those who teach this matter on its own:
A distinguishing mark made to be trampled, Rabba said:
It is not a distinguishing mark,
and Rava said: It is a distinguishing mark.

steps.triggerLanguage: כְּרִיכוֹת בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים – הֲרֵי אֵלּוּ שֶׁלּוֹ. אָמַר רַבָּה: וַאֲפִילּוּ בְּדָבָר שֶׁיֵּשׁ בּוֹ סִימָן. אַלְמָא קָסָבַר רַבָּה: סִימָן הֶעָשׂוּי לִידָּרֵס לָא הָוֵי סִימָן. רָבָא אָמַר: לֹא שָׁנוּ אֶלָּא בְּדָבָר שֶׁאֵין בּוֹ סִימָן, אֲבָל בְּדָבָר שֶׁיֵּשׁ בּוֹ סִימָן חַיָּיב לְהַכְרִיז. אַלְמָא קָסָבַר רָבָא: סִימָן הֶעָשׂוּי לִידָּרֵס הָוֵי סִימָן. וְאִיכָּא דְּמַתְנֵי לְהָא שְׁמַעְתָּא בְּאַנְפֵּי נַפְשַׁהּ: סִימָן הֶעָשׂוּי לִידָּרֵס, רַבָּה אָמַר: לָא הָוֵי סִימָן, וְרָבָא אָמַר: הָוֵי סִימָן.

rashi.hebrew: העשוי לידרס - שהמקום שהוא שם רגיל בדריסת בני אדם והחפץ נמוך ונוח לידרס:
rashi.english: Prone to being trampled - The place where the object is located is commonly trampled by people, and the object itself is low and likely to be stepped on.
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines the phrase העשוי לידרס as referring to an object located in a place commonly trampled by people, where the object itself is low and susceptible to being stepped on.

rashi.hebrew: לא הוי סימן - שאין בעליו סומך לתת בו סימן מימר אמר נשחת הסימן בדריסת הרגלים:
rashi.english: It is not considered a distinguishing mark- The owner does not rely on it as a mark. He assumes that any identifying mark has been erased due to being trampled by feet.
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבּה considers such a סימן invalid because the owner does not rely on it, assuming the mark would be destroyed by foot traffic.

steps.title: Challenge to רַבָּא from the בְּרֵיתָא on bundles
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara challenges Rava's position using a Baraita that distinguishes between bundles in a public domain (finder keeps) and a private domain (must proclaim).
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא brings a בְּרֵיתָא to test the dispute. It reasons that if the bundles lack a סִימָן, there is nothing to proclaim in a private domain. Therefore, the בְּרֵיתָא must refer to bundles with a סִימָן.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The גמרא assumes the בְּרֵיתָא discusses bundles with a סִימָן because otherwise, the obligation to proclaim in a private domain is inexplicable. If they have a סִימָן, and the finder keeps them in a public domain, it proves that a סִימָן prone to trampling is not valid. This is a direct תְּיוּבְתָּא of רַבָּא.
steps.whatToRemember: The challenge hinges on the assumption that the בְּרֵיתָא discusses bundles WITH a סִימָן.
steps.confusionAlert: The גמרא uses a logical deduction: if no סִימָן, what is proclaimed? Thus, there must be a סִימָן.
steps.whyThisMatters: This sets up the need for רַבָּא to explain the בְּרֵיתָא in a way that accommodates his view.

phrases.english: We learned: Bundles in a public domain –
these are his,
in a private domain –
he takes and proclaims. What is the case?

phrases.aramaic: אִי דְּלֵית בְּהוּ סִימָן, בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד מַאי מַכְרֵיז?
phrases.english: If there is no distinguishing mark, in a private domain, what does he proclaim?
Rather, is it not that there is a distinguishing mark,
and it teaches: In a public domain –
these are his.

phrases.aramaic: אַלְמָא סִימָן הֶעָשׂוּי לִידָּרֵס לָא הָוֵי סִימָן,
phrases.english: Apparently, a distinguishing mark that is made to be trampled is not a distinguishing mark,
a refutation of Rava!

steps.triggerLanguage: תְּנַן: כְּרִיכוֹת בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים – הֲרֵי אֵלּוּ שֶׁלּוֹ, בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד – נוֹטֵל וּמַכְרִיז. הֵיכִי דָּמֵי? אִי דְּלֵית בְּהוּ סִימָן, בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד מַאי מַכְרֵיז? אֶלָּא לָאו דְּאִית בְּהוּ סִימָן, וְקָתָנֵי: בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים – הֲרֵי אֵלּוּ שֶׁלּוֹ. אַלְמָא סִימָן הֶעָשׂוּי לִידָּרֵס לָא הָוֵי סִימָן, תְּיוּבְתָּא דְּרָבָא!

rashi.hebrew: ברה"י - כגון בשדה זרועה שאין רוב בני אדם דורכין בה ויש מיעוט שהולכין בה:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רשות היחיד refers to a field where most people do not walk, but a minority do.

rashi.hebrew: ה"ג אי דלית בהו סימן ברה"י מאי מכריז - כלומר כי מכריז זה מצא אבידה מאי מכריז שיוכל ליתן האובד סימן בה:
rashi.english: This is the correct text: "If the lost object has no distinguishing mark and was found on private property, what would the finder announce"? - Meaning, when the finder makes an announcement that he has found a lost item, what exactly would he announce that would allow the owner to provide a distinguishing sign for it?
rashi.takeaway: רש״י clarifies that the question 'what does he proclaim?' means that without a סימן, the owner cannot provide identifying details to claim the item.

tosafot.hebrew: אי דליכא סימן מאי מכריז - פירוש למה צריך להכריז המוצא מאחר שהאבוד אין בו סימן ליתן ומשני דזה מכריז שאותו שאבד יתן סימן מקום וכן לקמן (בבא מציעא דף כח.) דקאמר כדי שילך שלשה ויחזור שלשה ויכריז יום אחד היינו נמי שהמוצא מכריז והאובד יתן סימן אך קשה דבפרק אלו עוברין (פסחים דף מט: ושם ד"ה ויש) אמרינן ע"ה אין מכריזין על אבידתו והיאך אנו יודעין זה אם הוא של ע"ה ויש לומר דמיירי במוצא במקום דשכיח ע"ה ורש"י שפירש דהמוצא מכריז מקום שיאמר מצאתי חפץ במקום פלוני והוא יאמר חפץ פלוני ויקחנה ק"ק דהא תנן בפירקין (דף כח:) אמר אבידה ולא אמר סימנין לא אמר כלום אלמא במה שמכוין החפץ אינו סימן וי"ל דהתם נמי לאו משום שהוא חשוד לשקר שהפסיד חפץ אלא משום דא"ל כי היכי דאת אבדת חפץ ה"נ אימר אדם אחר הפסיד אבל הכא מהדרינן דאין רגילות ששני בני אדם אבדו חפץ זה כזה ובמקום אחד ולפרש"י הוה מכריז מקום דהכא פירושו ממש דומיא דמכריז מנין דשלהי פרק קמא (דף כ:):
tosafot.english: "If there is no identifying mark, what does he announce?" – This means: why should the finder need to make an announcement if the lost item has no identifying mark that the owner could provide? The Gemara answers that the finder announces the location where the item was found. Similarly, later (Bava Metzia 28a), when it is stated that one must walk three days forward and three days back and announce for one day, it also refers to the finder making an announcement, and the owner providing an identifying detail. However, there is a difficulty from the passage in Pesachim (49b), where it says that one does not announce the lost items of an am ha'aretz (an unlearned person). How, then, would we know whether the item belongs to an am ha’aretz? One could say that this applies in a place where amei ha’aretz are commonly found. Rashi explains that the finder announces the location, saying, “I found an item in such-and-such a place,” and the owner would then respond with “I lost such-and-such an item” and reclaim it. However, this is difficult, since we learned in our chapter (28a) that if one merely states, “I lost an item,” without providing any identifying marks, it is considered meaningless. This implies that merely identifying the object is not sufficient as proof of ownership. It can be answered that in that case (on 28a), the reason it is not accepted is not that we suspect the claimant of lying about losing an item, but rather because the finder could argue: “Just as you lost such an item, someone else may have lost the same kind of item.” However, in this case, we assume that it is uncommon for two people to lose an identical item in the same location. According to Rashi’s explanation, the meaning of “announcing the location” here should be understood literally, just as the announcement of the quantity of money found (mentioned at the end of the first chapter, 20a).
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות explains that the proclamation involves the finder announcing the location, allowing the owner to identify the item by stating what was lost there, and addresses a difficulty from פסחים regarding non-proclamation for an עובד כוכבים by suggesting it applies where they are common, while reconciling with the rule that merely stating 'I lost an item' is insufficient by noting that losing identical items in the same place is uncommon.

steps.title: רַבָּא's resolution: Proclaiming the מָקוֹם
steps.stepSummary: Rava resolves the challenge by asserting that the Baraita discusses bundles without a siman, and the proclamation in a private domain refers to the location (מָקוֹם).
steps.whatsHappening: רַבָּא rejects the assumption that the בְּרֵיתָא discusses bundles with a סִימָן. He argues that even without a סִימָן, one must proclaim in a private domain. The proclamation is about the location.
steps.deeperAnalysis: רַבָּא shifts the premise: the בְּרֵיתָא is about bundles WITHOUT a סִימָן. The obligation to proclaim in a private domain is fulfilled by describing the location. This works because רַבָּא holds that location IS a סִימָן, whereas רַבָּה holds it is not. This explains why רַבָּה says the finder keeps them in a public domain but must proclaim in a private domain.

keyTerms.meaning: Always / in all cases (used to reject a premise)

steps.whatToRemember: רַבָּא introduces the concept of מָקוֹם as a valid סִימָן for proclamation.
steps.confusionAlert: Note that רַבָּא holds מָקוֹם הָוֵי סִימָן, while רַבָּה holds מָקוֹם לָא הָוֵי סִימָן.
steps.whyThisMatters: This introduces a secondary dispute (location as סִימָן) that is crucial for the subsequent resolutions.

phrases.english: Rava said to you:
Always that there is no distinguishing mark in them,

phrases.aramaic: וּדְקָא אָמְרַתְּ בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד מַאי מַכְרֵיז? מַכְרֵיז מָקוֹם.
phrases.english: And that which you said: In a private domain, what does he proclaim? He proclaims the location.

phrases.aramaic: וְרַבָּה אָמַר: מָקוֹם לָא הָוֵי סִימָן.
phrases.english: And Rabba said: The location is not a distinguishing mark.
As it was stated: Regarding location, Rabba said:
It is not a distinguishing mark,
And Rava said: It is a distinguishing mark.

steps.triggerLanguage: אָמַר לָךְ רָבָא: לְעוֹלָם דְּלֵית בְּהוּ סִימָן, וּדְקָא אָמְרַתְּ בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד מַאי מַכְרֵיז? מַכְרֵיז מָקוֹם. וְרַבָּה אָמַר: מָקוֹם לָא הָוֵי סִימָן. דְּאִיתְּמַר: מָקוֹם, רַבָּה אָמַר: לָא הָוֵי סִימָן, וְרָבָא אָמַר: הָוֵי סִימָן.

rashi.hebrew: מכריז מקום - ואינו מכריז שם האבידה אלא שם המקום מי שאבדה ממנו אבידה במקום פלוני יבא ויאמר מה איבד וזה בא ואומר אבדתי שם חפץ פלוני:
rashi.english: He announces only the location and does not announce the specific lost item. Instead, he declares that someone lost an item in a particular place. [[The rightful owner]] will then come and specify what item was lost, saying, "I lost such-and-such an object there".
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that when the Gemara says the finder proclaims the location, it means he announces that an item was lost in a specific place, without describing the item itself, allowing the owner to identify it by stating what was lost there.

tosafot.hebrew: ורבה אמר מקום לא הוי סימן - ולפי דסבירא ליה לרבה מקום לא הוי סימן הוצרך לומר דסי' העשוי לידרס לא הוי סימן:
tosafot.takeaway: תוספות notes that since רבה holds that location is not a valid סימן, he must rely on the principle that a סימן intended to be trampled on is not considered a valid סימן to explain the Baraita's ruling.

steps.title: Challenge from the בְּרֵיתָא on הָאֲלוּמּוֹת
steps.stepSummary: The Gemara brings a Baraita that discusses both bundles (כְּרִיכוֹת) and sheaves (הָאֲלוּמּוֹת), creating a difficulty for both Rabba and Rava.
steps.whatsHappening: The גמרא cites a בְּרֵיתָא that distinguishes between bundles (finder keeps in public, proclaims in private) and הָאֲלוּמּוֹת (must proclaim in both). It asks how each אָמוֹרָא explains this בְּרֵיתָא according to their respective principles.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The בְּרֵיתָא introduces a new variable: הָאֲלוּמּוֹת, which require proclamation in both domains. This complicates the previous resolutions. רַבָּה must explain why הָאֲלוּמּוֹת require proclamation even in a public domain, and רַבָּא must explain why הָאֲלוּמּוֹת require proclamation even in a public domain where location might be valid. The גמרא sets up the detailed resolutions.
steps.whatToRemember: The בְּרֵיתָא adds הָאֲלוּמּוֹת, which are treated differently from bundles.
steps.confusionAlert: Note the distinction between כְּרִיכוֹת (bundles) and הָאֲלוּמּוֹת (sheaves/large bundles).
steps.whyThisMatters: This forces both אָמוֹרַיִם to refine their explanations to account for the different treatment of הָאֲלוּמּוֹת.

phrases.english: Come and hear: Bundles in a public domain –
these are his,
in a private domain –
he takes and proclaims. And the sheaves,

phrases.aramaic: בֵּין בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים בֵּין בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד –
phrases.english: whether in a public domain or in a private domain –
he takes and proclaims.

phrases.aramaic: רַבָּה הֵיכִי מְתָרֵץ לַהּ, וְרָבָא הֵיכִי מְתָרֵץ לַהּ?
phrases.english: How does Rabba answer it, and how does Rava answer it?
Rabba answers according to his reasoning, with a distinguishing mark.
And Rava answers according to his reasoning, with a location.

steps.triggerLanguage: תָּא שְׁמַע: כְּרִיכוֹת בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים – הֲרֵי אֵלּוּ שֶׁלּוֹ, בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד – נוֹטֵל וּמַכְרִיז. וְהָאֲלוּמּוֹת, בֵּין בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים בֵּין בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד – נוֹטֵל וּמַכְרִיז. רַבָּה הֵיכִי מְתָרֵץ לַהּ, וְרָבָא הֵיכִי מְתָרֵץ לַהּ? רַבָּה מְתָרֵץ לְטַעְמֵיהּ, בְּסִימָן. וְרָבָא מְתָרֵץ לְטַעְמֵיהּ, בְּמָקוֹם.

rashi.hebrew: והאלומות - עומרים גדולים:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י defines הָאֲלוּמּוֹת as large sheaves, distinguishing them from regular bundles.

rashi.hebrew: רבה היכי מתרץ לה - רבה דאמר טעמא דרה"ר משום דנדרס מאי שנא אלומות:
rashi.english: How does Rabba explain this - Rabba, who explained that the reason for objects found in a public domain [belonging to the finder] is that they get trampled, how does he differentiate between large bundles [and regular bundles]....
rashi.takeaway: רש״י frames the challenge to רבה: since רבה holds that items in רשות הרבים belong to the finder because they are trampled, how does he explain why large sheaves require proclamation?

rashi.hebrew: ורבא - דמוקים לכריכות כשאין בו סימן מאי שנא כריכות ומאי שנא אלומות:
rashi.english: And Rava, who establishes that tied bundles refer to cases where there is no distinguishing mark, what is the difference between these bundles and large bundles.
rashi.takeaway: רש״י frames the challenge to רבא: since רבא holds that bundles without a סימן belong to the finder, what is the distinction between regular bundles and large sheaves that requires proclamation?

rashi.hebrew: רבה מתרץ לטעמיה - דטעמא דרשות הרבים דכריכות משום סימן ובדבר שיש בה סימן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבה resolves the difficulty by maintaining his principle that the distinction depends on the presence of a סימן, implying large sheaves inherently possess a distinguishing mark.

rashi.hebrew: במקום - טעמא דכריכות ברשות הרבים משום מקום ובדבר שאין בו סימן:
rashi.takeaway: רש״י explains that רבא resolves the difficulty by maintaining his principle that the distinction depends on the location (מקום), implying large sheaves are found in a place that indicates ownership.

steps.title: רַבָּה's resolution: Trampling of marks
steps.stepSummary: Rabba explains the Baraita by focusing on whether the siman is trampled. Bundles in a public domain are trampled, destroying the siman. Sheaves are high and not trampled, so the siman remains.
steps.whatsHappening: רַבָּה resolves the ברייתא by applying his principle that a mark prone to trampling is invalid. In a public domain, bundles are trampled, invalidating the mark, so the finder keeps them; in a private domain, they are not trampled, so the mark is valid and proclamation is required.
steps.deeperAnalysis: The resolution hinges on physical reality: כְּרִיכוֹת are low and get trampled in a public domain, while הָאֲלוּמּוֹת are high and escape trampling. This perfectly aligns with רַבָּה's view that סִימָן הֶעָשׂוּי לִידָּרֵס לָא הָוֵי סִימָן.

phrases.english: Rabba answers according to his opinion in the mnemonic:
Bundles in a public domain –
These are his, because of trampling.

phrases.aramaic: בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד – נוֹטֵל וּמַכְרִיז, דְּלָא מִדַּרְסָא. וְהָאֲלוּמּוֹת,
phrases.english: In a private domain – he takes and proclaims, because it is not trampled. And the sheaves,

phrases.aramaic: בֵּין בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים וּבֵין בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד –
phrases.english: Whether in a public domain or in a private domain –

phrases.aramaic: נוֹטֵל וּמַכְרִיז, כֵּיוָן דִּגְבִיהָן לָא מִדַּרְסָא.
phrases.english: He takes and proclaims, since they are elevated, they are not trampled.

steps.triggerLanguage: רַבָּה מְתָרֵץ לְטַעְמֵיהּ בְּסִימָן: כְּרִיכוֹת בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים – הֲרֵי אֵלּוּ שֶׁלּוֹ, מִשּׁוּם דְּמִדַּרְסָא. בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד – נוֹטֵל וּמַכְרִיז, דְּלָא מִדַּרְסָא. וְהָאֲלוּמּוֹת, בֵּין בִּרְשׁוּת הָרַבִּים וּבֵין בִּרְשׁוּת הַיָּחִיד – נוֹטֵל וּמַכְרִיז, כֵּיוָן דִּגְבִיהָן לָא מִדַּרְסָא.
